exits.label: ◂ back down to the almshouse line

npcs.name: Bregan
npcs.title: a soldier still holding the post
npcs.hail: *(At the broken gate of a fortress four hundred years fallen stands a soldier who has not left his post. Bregan holds a spear gone to rust-coloured shadow, his eyes on the dark beyond the wall, and he challenges you the instant you draw near — sharp, drilled, a man at his work. He was set to guard this gate in the last siege of a keep whose name no living tongue remembers, given the oldest order a soldier knows — hold until relieved — and he held, and the keep fell around him, and he died at the gate with the order still binding him, and no relief ever came, because everyone who could have sent it was dead or fled. Four hundred years, and he holds it yet, because a soldier does not abandon a post, and because — your cold reads it under the discipline — if he leaves, and the war is over, and the gate guards nothing, then his death was for nothing, and that is the one assault Bregan cannot survive.)*

npcs.name: Cael
npcs.title: the last soul the watch protects
npcs.hail: *(Just inside the gate, in the lee of the wall where the watch-fire's memory still throws a little warmth, sits a soul the sentinel does not know is dead. Cael was one of the townsfolk who sheltered in the keep when the siege came — farmers, children, the old — behind the gate Bregan held, and when the keep fell they died with it, and over the long centuries the others moved on or dissolved one by one, until only Cael remains. Cael knows the danger passed four hundred years ago. Cael knows the gate guards nothing now. But Cael stays, in the lee of the wall, for one reason only: because Bregan will not leave his post, and Cael cannot bear to let the soldier keep his lonely watch with no one left behind the gate to keep it for. They are each other's cage, and each the other's only company, and neither will be the first to go.)*

npcs.name: Captain Wystan
npcs.title: the one who never sent relief
npcs.hail: *(Apart from the gate, at the edge of the light, where he has stood for four hundred years unable to come closer, lingers an officer's shade. Captain Wystan gave Bregan his order — hold until relieved — and meant to send the relief within the hour, and then the outer wall broke and the captain was carried back in the rout and cut down a mile away with the order undelivered and the relief never mustered. He has spent the whole of his death within sight of the post he doomed and has never once crossed to it, because to face Bregan is to face the man he left to hold a gate forever on a promise he failed to keep. He holds the one thing that could free the sentinel by every law a soldier honours — the authority to stand him down — and he cannot make himself carry it the last hundred feet, because giving the order means saying aloud, at last, I did not come for you.)*

variants.text: *(He brings the rusted spear across his body and his voice cracks out like an order, four hundred years sharp.)* …*Halt.* *(he sights down the dark at you)* Name and business. This gate is *held.* *(he does not lower the spear)* …You're a *cold* one. Dead-touched. *Not* the *enemy*, then — they were *living*, all of them, the *last* time anything came up that road. *(a fraction of the tension eases, but he does not stand easy)* …But I'll *tell* you what I told the *last* shade that came *talking*, and the one before: I am *Bregan*, sworn of the Keep guard, and I am *under orders.* *Hold* the gate *until relieved.* *(his jaw sets)* The keep fell. I know it fell — I *felt* it fall, around me, the walls and the screaming and then the *quiet.* I *died* here, at this *gate*, with the order *on* me. And the order does not *lift* when you *die*, cold one. It lifts when you are *relieved.* *(barely, under the iron)* …No one has *come* to relieve me. In a *long* while. A *long*, *long* while. *(the spear wavers, steadies)* …So I *hold.* That is the *whole* of it. I *hold* the *gate.* If you've come to *talk* me *off* it — *save* your *breath.* A soldier who *abandons* a post he was told to *hold* is *nothing.* And I will *not* die having been *nothing.* I *held.* That's — *(quietly)* — that's all I've *got* left to *be.* The one who *held.*

nodes.speaker: Bregan
nodes.text: *(He stands at parade-rest now, the spear grounded, but his eyes never quite leave the dark road beyond the gate — the discipline of four centuries, watching for an enemy four centuries gone.)* …*Speak* your piece, cold one. *(evenly)* I'll hear it. A watch is *long*, and *talk* passes it. But mark me — *talk* is *all* it'll be. I do not *leave* the *gate.* Not for *coaxing*, not for *pity*, not for a *cold* soul's *clever* words. *Only* for *relief*, *properly* *given.* That's the *law.* I've *kept* it four hundred years. I'll keep it four hundred more.

choices.text: *(read the true history of this post — what it was, what it guarded, what became of it)* "Stand easy a moment, Bregan. Let me read what this gate was, and what it held."
"What are you holding the gate *against*, Bregan? The enemy's four hundred years dead. The keep is dust. Who do you think is still coming up that road?"

*(the one who knows a soul isn't its post)* "Bregan — hear me, soldier, because I died and came back and I learned the thing that can stand a man down at last. You're holding because you think if the war's over and the gate guards nothing, your death meant nothing. It isn't true. For four hundred years one soul has sheltered behind this gate and not been alone — because *you stood faithful.* That's not a post that guarded *nothing*. That's the realest duty there is: that someone was *kept.* Your watch isn't being abandoned. It's *complete.* You didn't fail to be relieved — you outlasted the need for it. Stand down not because it was pointless, but because it is *finished*, and *won.*"

*(stand the watch through to its end with him, and then give the stand-down with the only authority that's left — the authority of the last soul who'll ever come up that road)* "Then let me relieve you properly, Bregan. I'm the last thing that will ever come up this road, and I've come to take the watch. Stand down, soldier. The gate is mine now. You're relieved."
"The one you guard — Cael — is still behind this gate, Bregan. And Cael stays only because *you* stay. You're each other's cage. The watch ends when you leave it *together*, not before."
"Captain Wystan is here, Bregan. The one who gave you the order. He's come at last to give you another."
"Carry on, soldier. I'll have a look around the post."

nodes.speaker: Bregan
nodes.text: *(You let your cold read the long history written into the stones of the gate, and it comes — the whole sad arc of it, clearer to you than to the man who lived and died in it.)* *(This was the Keep of a border lord in a war whose cause is forgotten, in a kingdom that fell so long ago it left no name in any living book. The siege was the last of three; the outer wall broke at dawn; the lord and his knights died in the yard; and the townsfolk — the farmers and the old and the children who'd fled inside the walls for safety — crowded into the inner bailey behind the one gate still held, the gate where a guardsman named Bregan stood with an order to hold until relieved. He held it the whole long day, alone after the others fell, while behind him the sheltered souls waited for a relief that the breaking of the line had already made impossible. He held it until he died. And here is the thing your cold reads that Bregan never knew: he held it long enough. The enemy, having taken the yard and the lord, never pressed the inner gate — there was no plunder left worth the cost — and withdrew at dusk. The townsfolk behind Bregan's gate were not put to the sword. They died, in the end, of the long years and the dark like all the dead die — but they died unafraid, and not alone, because to the very last a soldier stood between them and the night. The post did not fail. The post was the one thing in the whole lost keep that held.)*

nodes.speaker: Bregan
nodes.text: *(The question lands oddly, and for the first time the drilled certainty gutters, like a torch in a draught.)* …What am I holding it *against.* *(slowly)* …The *enemy.* The — *(he stares down the dark road, and his brow furrows)* …They wore — *(he reaches for it)* …I don't — *(quietly, troubled)* …Do you know, cold one, I cannot *rightly* *call* their *colours* anymore. The *enemy.* I have *held* this gate against them for — for *longer* than I can *count*, and I cannot *remember* their *banner.* *(the spear dips)* …But that *doesn't* — that *changes* *nothing.* The order wasn't *'hold* until the *enemy* *stops* *coming.'* The order was *'hold* until *relieved.'* *(stubborn, but shaken)* …Whether *anyone's* *coming* up that road or *not* is *not* a *guardsman's* to *judge.* My *business* is the *gate.* Held or not held. *(barely)* …Though I'll *grant* you this much, cold one, since you *asked* it *plain* — it has been a *very* *long* time since *anything* came up that road but *shades*, and *talkers*, and *you.* *(he grips the spear harder, as if to stop the doubt)* …But a *post* you *stop* *holding* the *moment* it *seems* *quiet* is a *post* that *falls* the *one* *night* you're *wrong.* I'll not *gamble* the *gate* on the *war* being *over.* I *gambled* once that a *wall* would *hold* till *morning.* It didn't. *(quietly)* I don't *gamble* *walls* anymore.

nodes.speaker: Bregan
nodes.text: *(The spear comes slowly off his shoulder, and he stares at you, and the iron of four hundred years cracks down its whole length at once.)* …*Complete.* *(barely)* …Not — not *abandoned.* *(his voice shakes)* …*Won.* *(he looks back, over his shoulder, toward the lee of the wall where a soul has sat the whole long while)* …Someone was *kept.* *(the words come hard, like a man learning to breathe a new air)* …I thought — cold one, I thought the *whole* *worth* of me was in the *holding*, and that if the *holding* ever *stopped* mattering then *I* stopped — that I'd be just a *fool* who *stood* at a *dead* gate *guarding* *dust* for *four* *hundred* *years.* *(his eyes are wet)* …But you're saying the gate was *never* dust. That *behind* it, the *whole* time, there was a *soul* who was *not* *alone* *because* I *stood.* *(he grips the spear, then, deliberately, grounds it — a different gesture than before, not readiness but rest)* …That's — *(barely)* …that's a *post* worth the *holding*, cold one. That's the *only* post that was *ever* worth a *thing.* Not the *gate.* The *keeping.* *(he straightens, and for the first time looks not like a sentry but like a man)* …Then I am *relieved.* Not by *orders.* By the *truth* that the watch is *over* because it *did* what a watch is *for.* *(quietly, the weight of centuries setting down)* …Tell me one thing, cold one, and tell me *true*, soldier to soul. The one I *kept* — the one behind the *gate.* Are they — are they *still* there? Because I'll not stand *down* and leave a *kept* soul *alone* in the *dark.* I'll *die* a *fool* at this *gate* *first.* *Where* *is* the *one* I *held* *for?*

nodes.speaker: Bregan
nodes.text: *(You do not coax him and you do not argue. You square up, and you take the watch — you tell him, in the plain flat cadence of the duty itself, that you are the last thing that will ever come up that road, that you have come to take the gate, that he is relieved — and four hundred years of a soldier's whole soul, drilled to one word, hears the word it has waited the entire span of its death to hear, and answers before doubt can.)* *(Bregan brings the rusted spear up in a salute so crisp it is almost a sound, and holds it, and his eyes shine.)* …*Watch* *relieved.* *(his voice breaks on the formula and steadies on it both at once)* …*Post* *passed*, *sound* and *held*, to the *cold* one, this — this *whatever-hour* it is, gods, I've *lost* the *count* of *hours.* *(he completes the salute, and lets it fall, and the falling is the most enormous thing you have ever watched a soul do)* …*Four* *hundred* *years*, cold one, I *prayed* for those *two* *words* and I'd *stopped* *believing* they'd *ever* *come.* *'You're* *relieved.'* *(he laughs, a wet, wrecked, soldier's laugh)* …Do you *know* — *nobody* ever *has* to *be* told *twice.* The *body* just — *knows.* The *order* *lifts* and the *weight* of the *whole* *war* goes *off* your *shoulders* at *once* and you *remember* you have *shoulders*, that you were a *man* before you were a *post.* *(he steps — actually steps — back from the gate, the first step in four centuries)* …I'll go *fetch* the one I *kept.* We'll *go* *together.* A *guardsman* *carries* his *charge* *out.* *That's* the *last* of the *order*, and the *only* part I'll *gladly* *keep.* *Bless* you, *relief.* You came. *Late* — *(a grin)* — *gods*, you came *late.* But you *came.*

nodes.speaker: Bregan
nodes.text: *(You take the watch — tell him you've come to relieve him, that the gate is yours now, that he's stood down — and you see it reach him, the word he's waited for, even if your authority to give it is a thing you've claimed rather than carry. He wavers on the edge of accepting it, a soldier's whole training leaning toward the order, a soldier's whole caution holding back.)* …*Relieved.* *(slowly, testing the word)* …By *you.* *(he studies your cold face)* …You're *not* of the *Keep* guard, cold one. You've no *colours* I *know.* By the *strict* of it, you've *no* more *right* to *stand* me *down* than the *last* *shade* that *tried.* *(but the spear lowers, by degrees)* …And *yet.* *(quietly)* …You said the *one* *true* thing: that you're the *last* that'll *ever* come up this *road.* And a *relief* that *never* *comes* and a *relief* with *no* *proper* *colours* — *(he wrestles it)* — at *some* point, cold one, a soldier has to *reckon* that the *one* who *finally* *comes* is the *relief* he's *getting*, *colours* or *no*, or he *holds* a *dead* gate *forever* on a *technicality.* *(he grounds the spear)* …I'll — I'll *take* it. *Provisionally.* *(a rough almost-smile)* …Stand *with* me a *while* first, would you? Make it — make it *feel* like a *proper* *hand-over*, not a *desertion* I've *talked* myself into. I've *held* this *gate* too *long* to leave it like a *thief* in the *night.* *Help* me leave it like a *soldier.* And then — *(barely)* — then I'll go. I *will.* It's just — *four* *hundred* *years*, cold one. The *legs* are *slow* to *believe* the *watch* is *done.*

nodes.speaker: Bregan
nodes.text: *(You give the stand-down — but you give it as a soul giving an order, not a soldier passing a watch, and Bregan's whole drilled life knows the difference the way a smith knows true steel from soft. The spear comes back up.)* …*No.* *(flatly)* …You don't — you said the *words*, cold one, but you said them like a *civilian* *playing* at *soldier.* *(he sights down the gate again)* A *relief* isn't *words.* It's a *form.* The *out-going* *watch* names the *post*, the *threat*, the *standing* *orders*; the *relief* *repeats* them *back*, so *both* know the *gate* is *truly* *passed* and *nothing's* *dropped.* You don't *know* the *form.* You don't know what you'd be *taking* *on.* *(grimly)* …I'll *not* hand a *kept* *gate* to a soul that doesn't even know to *ask* what it *guards.* That's how *posts* *fall* — in the *sloppy* *hand-over*, the *thing* *unsaid.* *(he settles back to his watch)* …*Come* *back* when you can *take* it *proper*, cold one. *Ask* me the *standing* *orders.* *Learn* the *post.* *Then* I'll believe you've *come* to *hold* it and not just to *talk* me *off* it. A *gate's* not *abandoned* by *argument.* It's *passed*, *hand* to *hand*, *sound.*

nodes.speaker: Bregan
nodes.text: *(Reaching for the soldier's idiom you misjudge it badly — you order him to stand down *because the war's lost, the keep's dust, it was all for nothing, come away* — and you mean it as mercy, but to Bregan you have just named his deepest terror as plain fact, and a man does not abandon a post to the soul that tells him it was meaningless.)* …*Was* it. *(very quietly, and the quiet is worse than a shout)* …For *nothing.* *(the spear does not waver now; it is perfectly, terribly still)* …Then I will hold it *forever*, cold one. *(flatly)* You've *answered* your *own* *coaxing.* If the gate was for *nothing* — if I *stand* here a *fool* over *dust* — then the *holding* is the *only* *scrap* of *meaning* I have *left*, and you'll not *take* *that* from me too, and call it a *kindness.* *(he turns his face back to the dark road)* …*Go.* *(barely)* …You came to *lift* the *weight* and instead you *named* it *unbearable* and asked me to *carry* it *home.* *No.* The *gate* I can *hold.* *'It* was *all* for *nothing'* I *cannot.* So I'll *hold* the *gate*, and *not* *think* on the *other*, the way I've *not* *thought* on it for *four* *hundred* *years.* *(grimly)* …*Don't* come *back* with *that* one, cold one. *Anything* but *that* one. Tell me it *mattered* or tell me *nothing*, but do *not* tell me it was *waste* and expect me to *walk* *into* the *dark* *agreeing.*

nodes.speaker: Bregan
nodes.text: *(He turns fully from the gate now — the first time he has put his back to that dark road in your presence — and looks toward the lee of the wall, and his weathered face does something complicated and raw.)* …*Cael.* *(barely)* …Stays because *I* stay. *(slowly)* …I *knew* — cold one, I *knew* there was a *soul* back there, in the *lee.* I've — I've *talked* to them, across the *years.* *Kept* them *company* on the *long* *nights.* I *thought* — *(his voice catches)* …I thought I was *guarding* them. That my *holding* the *gate* was *keeping* them *safe.* *(the realization breaks over him)* …But you're telling me there's *nothing* to *keep* them *safe* *from.* That the *danger's* *centuries* *gone.* And they *stay* — not because they *need* my *gate* — but because they *won't* *leave* *me* *holding* it *alone.* *(he presses a hand to his face)* …*Gods.* We've been — we've been *guarding* *each* *other.* *Both* of us *free* to *go*, *both* of us *staying* *only* because the *other* *stays.* *Four* *hundred* *years.* *(a wrecked laugh)* …What a *pair* of *soldiers* we'd have made, cold one — *each* dying at our *post* to *cover* the *other's* *retreat*, and *neither* *retreating*, so *neither* *covered*, so *both* just — *standing.* *Forever.* *Loyal* to *death* and *past* it. *(he straightens, fierce now)* …Then we *go* *together.* That's the *only* *order* that *frees* us *both.* I'll *not* leave the gate till *Cael* will leave the *lee* — and *Cael* won't leave the *lee* till *I* leave the *gate* — so *bring* us *together*, cold one, and we'll take the *first* *step* at the *same* *moment*, the way it *should've* *been* from the *first.* *Go.* *Tell* *Cael* the *guard's* *ready* to *come* *off* the *wall* — if they'll *come* out of the *lee* to *walk* *beside* him.

nodes.speaker: Bregan
nodes.text: *(At the name — Captain Wystan — Bregan goes rigid, and his head turns toward the edge of the light where the officer's shade has stood apart for four hundred years, and his face floods with something that is not anger, which is the most heartbreaking part of it.)* …The *Captain.* *(barely)* …*Here.* He's — he's been *here?* *(his voice is very quiet)* …*All* this *while?* *(and then, the thing that undoes you, because there is no accusation in it, only a soldier's terrible gladness)* …Then he *didn't* *fall* on the *road.* I always — I *feared* the *Captain* *fell*, that the *relief* never *came* because there was *no* one *left* to *send* it. *(a rough breath)* …But he's *here.* He *made* it. *(he doesn't yet understand, and you haven't the heart, in this moment, to be the one)* …He's come to *give* the *order.* *(he straightens, and squares his shoulders, and four hundred years of waiting comes to attention)* …*Then* I'll *take* it from *him.* *Properly.* From the *one* who *gave* me the *first* one. *(quietly, fierce)* …Cold one — *go* and *tell* the *Captain* his *guardsman* is *still* at his *post.* Still *holding.* Like he *said.* *(barely)* …And tell him — tell him I'm *not* *angry.* Whatever *kept* him, whatever *broke*, four hundred years is a *long* time to *carry* a *thing*, and I'll *not* add to *his* *weight* by *being* *cold* when he *finally* *comes.* *Bring* him to the *gate.* I've been *waiting* for my *Captain.* I'd *like* — *(his voice breaks)* — I'd *like* to *see* him, before we *both* *stand* *down.*

nodes.speaker: Bregan
nodes.text: *(He returns to his watch, spear grounded, eyes to the dark road, the posture as natural to him now as breathing once was.)* …*Aye.* *Look* *round.* *(evenly)* *Mind* the *crates* — *(a flicker of dry soldier's humour)* — there's *four* *hundred* *years* of *nothing* in them, but *check* them *anyway.* That's the *watch.* You *check* the *quiet* *corners* *precisely* *because* nothing's *ever* in them, until the *once* something *is.* *(he settles)* …Cold one. *(quietly, as you go)* …That soul in the *lee* of the *wall.* *Cael.* *(he doesn't look away from the road)* …*Mind* them, would you, while you're about. They've — they've kept me *company* a *long* *while*, and I *worry* — *(gruffly)* — a *guardsman* *worries*, it's the *trade* — I worry they stay in the *cold* *lee* on *my* *account.* If you can *coax* them somewhere *warmer*, somewhere *on*, do it. *(barely)* …Don't *tell* them I asked. A *post* doesn't *fret* over its *charges* out *loud.* But — *see* to them. They're the — *(he stops, and what he doesn't say is the whole of it)* …they're the *reason*. Whatever the *reason* is. They're it.

conversations.title: Cael, in the Lee of the Wall

variants.text: *(The soul in the lee of the wall looks up as you approach, and unlike the sentinel there is no challenge in them, only the quiet, settled sadness of someone who made their peace with a small sorrow a very long time ago and has stopped expecting it to change.)* …Oh — hello. *(a tired, kind smile)* You got *past* Bregan, then. He must *like* you. He doesn't *challenge* the ones he *likes* more than *twice.* *(they shift to make room, an old courtesy)* …I'm Cael. I — *(a glance toward the sentinel at the gate)* — I *shelter* here. Have done a *long* time. *(quietly)* …You'll have *guessed*, if you're the dead-touched sort, that there's *nothing* to *shelter* *from* anymore. The siege ended four *hundred* years ago, cold one. The enemy went *home.* The *others* who hid here with me — my *neighbours*, the *children*, old *Maddoc* the *miller* — they *moved* *on*, one by one, down the *years*, the way the dead *do* when they've *made* their *peace.* *(a small, sad shrug)* …I made *mine* *ages* past. I could *go* *tomorrow.* The *road* on is — I can *feel* it, the *warm* way, the *rest.* *(they look at the sentinel again, and the sadness deepens)* …But there's *Bregan.* Still at the *gate.* Still *holding.* *Still* *waiting* for a *relief* that *isn't* *coming.* *(barely)* …And I *can't.* I can't just *walk* *off* down the *warm* road and leave that *good* man *standing* *guard* over an *empty* *lee* for *eternity*, *thinking* he's *keeping* someone *safe.* So I — I *stay.* So he's *guarding* *someone.* So the *holding* still *means* something. *(quietly)* …It's the *only* *gift* I've *got* left to *give* him. I *let* him *guard* me. Forever, if that's how *long* he *needs* to.*

nodes.speaker: Cael
nodes.text: *(They fold their hands and wait, patient as only the long-resigned are patient.)* …*Ask*, if you like, cold one. *(a faint smile)* I've *time.* Gods know I've *time.* It's the *one* thing this *lee* has *never* run *short* of.

choices.text: "Tell me who you were, Cael. Before the siege, before the lee. The life behind the waiting."
"You stay for him; he stays for you. Neither of you needs this gate anymore — you're each other's cage, Cael, and you've both mistaken it for duty."

*(the one who knows a watch kept out of love is no prison — until it is)* "Cael, hear me. The watch Bregan keeps for you isn't a cage — it started as the truest thing there is, two souls each staying so the other won't be alone. But love that can only hold still becomes a prison the day there's a door. There *is* a door. You can both walk out of it — *together.* Tell him you're ready to go, and you'll give him the one thing he's missing: somewhere to stand down *to*. He can't leave a post with a soul still behind it. So come out from behind it, and walk beside him instead."
"There's an officer apart from the gate — Captain Wystan, the one who never sent Bregan's relief. He carries the order that could free him. Did you know he was here?"
"Rest a while, Cael. I'll see what can be done for you both."

nodes.speaker: Cael
nodes.text: *(They smile, and the old life comes into their face like sun through a long-shuttered window.)* …*Before.* *(softly)* …I kept *bees*, cold one. *(a small laugh at your surprise)* *Bees.* Out past the east wall, in the good *clover* years. *Six* *hives.* I was *known* for it — *Cael's* *honey*, they'd say at the *market*, the *dark* kind, from the *heather* up the *hill.* *(the smile fades, gently)* …When the *war* came I drove the *hives* inside the walls on a *cart* — *fool* that I was, *bees* in a *siege* — and they *died*, of course, no *clover* behind *stone*, but I *couldn't* *leave* them, they were — they were *mine* to *keep.* *(they look at their hands)* …I think that's *why* I *understand* Bregan, cold one. We're the *same* *make*, him and me. *Keepers.* He *keeps* a *gate*; I *kept* *bees.* And a *keeper* doesn't *stop* *keeping* just because the *thing's* gone *hopeless.* You *keep* *past* *all* *sense.* It's *who* you *are.* *(quietly)* …So when I saw that *good* soldier still at his *post*, still *keeping*, with *no* one *left* to *keep* — I *understood* him *bone*-deep, and I *couldn't* *walk* away and *leave* a *fellow* *keeper* with *nothing* *left* to *keep.* So I *gave* him *me.* *Something* to *guard.* *(a sad smile)* …It's the *same* as the *bees*, really. You *can't* *save* them. But you *can't* *leave* them, either. So you *stay*, and you *keep*, and you call the *staying* *love*, because that's the *truest* *name* it has.

nodes.speaker: Cael
nodes.text: *(They are quiet a long moment, and when they answer there is no surprise in it, only the weary recognition of a truth long known and long avoided.)* …A *cage.* *(softly)* …*Yes.* *(they don't flinch from it)* …I've *known* that, cold one. For — oh, a *long* time. *(they look toward the gate)* The *kind* lie I *tell* myself is that I *stay* for *him*, *freely*, a *gift.* And that's — that's *half* *true.* *(barely)* …But the *other* half is *this*: I'm *frightened* to *go* *alone.* The *road* *on*, the *warm* way, the *rest* — it means *leaving*, and *leaving* means the *dark* between *here* and *there*, and I have *sat* in this *lee* so *long* that the *thought* of *walking* *out* of it by *myself* — *(they shiver)* …So I *tell* myself I *stay* for *Bregan.* And *Bregan* *tells* himself he *holds* for *me.* And *neither* of us has to *face* the *walking-out-alone*, because we've *roped* our *staying* to *each* *other* and called it *duty* and called it *love* and it *is* *both* — *(quietly)* — but it's *also* just *two* *frightened* *souls* who've made a *home* of *not-leaving.* *(they look at you)* …You're the *first* to *say* it *plain*, cold one. *Each* *other's* *cage.* *(a long breath)* …I think I've been *waiting* for someone to *say* it. Because *I* can't say it to *him* — it'd sound like I was *blaming* him for *trapping* me, when the *truth* is we *trapped* *each* *other*, *gently*, out of *love* and *fear* in *equal* *measure.* *(barely)* …*Can* you *un-trap* us, cold one? *Without* either of us feeling *blamed?* Because I *do* want *out.* I've wanted *out* for a *century.* I just — I want *him* to come *too.* I'll not buy my *freedom* with his *eternity.*

nodes.speaker: Cael
nodes.text: *(They take it in slowly, and then something that has been bowed in them for four hundred years begins, by degrees, to straighten.)* …Love that can only *hold* *still.* *(softly)* …becomes a *prison* the *day* there's a *door.* *(they look at you, eyes bright)* …And there *is* a *door.* There's *always* *been* a *door.* We've just both been *standing* with our *backs* to it, *guarding* *each* *other* from *the* *one* *thing* that *isn't* a *threat* — the *way* *out.* *(a wet laugh)* …I can give him *somewhere* to *stand* *down* *to.* *(they rise, the first time you've seen them leave the lee's lee)* …*Yes.* *That's* — cold one, that's the *thing* I could *never* *see* from *inside* the *fear*: that my *leaving* wouldn't *abandon* him to an *empty* *gate* — it would *release* him *from* it, because he only *holds* *for* *me*, and if I'm *walking* *out* the *door* then there's *no* one *left* to *hold* *for*, and he can *finally*, *honourably*, *come* *off* the *wall.* *(fierce now, decided)* …*Together.* We walk out *together.* I'll come out from behind his *gate* and he'll come *down* off his *post* and we'll take the *warm* road *side* by *side*, two *keepers* who *kept* *faith* with *each* *other* to the *very* *last* and are *done*, *now*, *gloriously* *done.* *(they grip your cold hand)* …*Take* me to him. Or — *no.* *Bring* him *word* first. Tell *Bregan* that the *one* he *guarded* is *ready* to *leave* the *lee* — and will, the *moment* he's *ready* to *leave* the *gate.* *Neither* *first.* *Both* *together.* That's the *only* *way* two *keepers* ever *lay* a *watch* *down*: at the *same* *breath*, so *neither* *feels* they *broke* it.

nodes.speaker: Cael
nodes.text: *(Their eyes widen, and they look toward the edge of the light where the officer's shade stands apart, and something between pity and old anger moves across their face.)* …*Him.* *(quietly)* …I *wondered* who that was. The shade that *stands* *apart* and *never* *comes* *close.* *(they study Wystan from across the dark)* …The one who *never* sent the *relief.* *(a long breath)* …I'll tell you a *hard* thing, cold one, and I'm — I'm not *proud* of it. For a *long* time, in the *early* years, I *hated* that shape in the dark, *whoever* it was. Because *someone* *doomed* Bregan to this *gate* — *someone* *gave* the *order* and *didn't* *come* *back* — and the *good* soldier has spent *four* *hundred* *years* *paying* for *another* *man's* *broken* *promise.* *(quietly)* …But *hatred's* *heavy*, and I *set* it *down* *ages* past. And *now* you tell me the man's *right* *there* — been *right* *there* the *whole* *while* — *(realization)* …*paying* too. In his *own* way. *Standing* *apart*, *unable* to *come* *close*, *guarding* *nothing* but his *own* *guilt.* *(softly)* …*Three* of us, then. Not *two.* *Three* souls *frozen* round this *gate* — the *guard* who *won't* *leave* the *post*, the *keeper* who *won't* *leave* the *guard*, and the *captain* who *can't* *face* *either.* *(she looks at you)* …If you mean to *free* us, cold one, you'll *not* do it *leaving* *him* in the *dark.* Bregan would *not* *want* that. I *know* that *man* — he'd *not* walk to his *rest* and leave his *Captain* *standing* *guilty* in the *cold.* *(barely)* …*All* *three*, cold one. Or it *won't* *take.* We came to this *gate* *together*, in a *war* — *guard*, *guarded*, and the *one* who *sent* us. We'll *leave* it *together*, or *not* at *all.*

nodes.speaker: Cael
nodes.text: *(They settle back into the lee, but they keep their eyes on you now, a flicker of something they'd thought long dead — hope — moving in them.)* …I'll be *here.* *(a faint smile)* …*Obviously.* *(they glance toward the gate, fond and aching)* …Cold one — *(as you turn)* …*whatever* you *do* with *Bregan.* Be — be *gentle* about the *guarding-nothing* part. *(quietly)* He's *built* his *whole* *self* on *being* the *one* who *held.* If you take that from him *wrong* — *too* *fast*, *too* *cold*, *'it* was *all* *for* *nothing,* old *man'* — you won't *free* him, you'll *break* him, and he'll *hold* that gate *out* of *spite* and *shame* till the *stars* *go* *out.* *(barely)* …Tell him it *mattered.* Because it *did.* *Gods*, cold one, it *did* — he kept a *frightened* soul *company* through *four* *hundred* *years* of *dark*, and *that's* — *(their voice catches)* …*that's* not *nothing.* That's the *most* *something* there *ever* *was.* *Tell* him that. *That's* the *relief* he *needs.* Not *'stand* *down.'* *'It* *mattered.'* *Those* are the *only* *two* *words* that'll *ever* get that *man* off that *wall.*

conversations.title: Captain Wystan, Apart from the Gate

variants.text: *(The officer's shade does not face the gate; he stands half-turned from it, as he has stood for four hundred years, and when he speaks to you his voice has the hollow precision of a man who has rehearsed his own condemnation so many times it has worn smooth.)* …You've been to the *gate.* *(he does not ask)* …To *Bregan.* *(his jaw tightens)* …Still *holding.* Of *course* he is. I *gave* him the *order.* *(barely)* …I am *Wystan.* I had the *captaincy* of the *Keep* guard, that last *season.* And when the *enemy* came to the *walls* I set my *men* at the *posts*, and to the *gate* of the *inner* *bailey* — the *last* *gate*, the one the *townsfolk* *sheltered* behind — I set *Bregan*, who was *steady*, and *young*, and *trusted* me. *(his voice is flat, controlled, terrible)* …*'Hold* until *relieved*,*'* I told him. And I *meant* it — I meant to *send* the *second* *watch* within the *hour*, to *spell* him, it was *routine* — and then the *outer* *wall* *broke*, and the *yard* was *chaos*, and I was *swept* *back* in the *rout* and *cut* *down* a *mile* from the *keep* with the *relief* *un-mustered* and the *order* *un-lifted.* *(he finally looks toward the gate, and away again, fast, as if it burns)* …He has *held* that *gate* *four* *hundred* *years*, cold one, *waiting* for a *relief* I *promised* and *never* *sent.* …And I have *stood* *here*, in *sight* of him, the *whole* of my *death*, and I have *never* *once* been able to *walk* the *hundred* *feet* and *tell* him *why.*

nodes.speaker: Captain Wystan
nodes.text: *(He stands rigid, eyes on the dark, the gate at the edge of his vision a thing he will not turn to look at full.)* …*Say* it, then. *(quietly)* …Whatever you've come to say. I have *heard* every *accusation* there *is*, cold one — I have *made* them *all*, *against* *myself*, *ten* *thousand* *times*, *standing* *here.* There is *nothing* you can *name* that I have not *already* *carved* into my *own* *soul.* *(barely)* …But say it. A *captain* *hears* the *charge.*

choices.text: "Tell me the whole of it, Wystan. The wall breaking, the rout. I'd understand how a relief comes to be un-sent — not to excuse it, but to know it true."
"You could free him with three words, Wystan — 'watch relieved, soldier.' You've had four hundred years to walk over and say them. Why won't you?"

*(the one who knows the order frees the giver too)* "Wystan — hear me, Captain, because I've carried more guilt than you'd guess and learned its one cure. You think walking to that gate means confessing you failed him, and you can't bear to. But Bregan doesn't want your guilt. He wants his *relief* — and you are the one soul in any world with the authority to give it. The order won't damn you. It will free you both: him from the post, you from the hundred feet you've never crossed. He's not waiting to accuse you. He's waiting for his Captain. Go give the order you've owed him for four hundred years. It's the only thing that lifts it — off him, and off you."
"He's not angry, Wystan. I've come from the gate. Bregan feared *you* fell on the road — and he's *glad* you didn't. He's waiting to see his Captain before he stands down."
"Stand with your guilt a while longer, Captain. I'll be back."

nodes.speaker: Captain Wystan
nodes.text: *(He tells it like a man reading a charge-sheet, every fact in its place, sparing himself nothing.)* …Dawn. The *third* day. *(flatly)* …The *outer* wall was *old* — we'd *told* the *lord* it wanted *mortar*, *years* running, and there was *never* the *coin.* It came *down* at the *north* *tower* under the *third* *ram*, all at *once*, more than any of us *reckoned* — a *whole* *span* of it, *gone*, and the *enemy* *poured* in *faster* than the *bell* could *ring* it. *(his hands close)* …I was on the *south* *wall.* By the *time* I'd *crossed* to the *yard* it was *lost* — *knots* of *men* *dying* in *clumps*, the *lord* already *down*, *no* *line* left to *hold.* And I *should* have — *(barely)* …I *had* a *moment*, cold one. *One.* When I could have *run* to the *inner* *gate* and *told* *Bregan* *'fall* *back*, *bring* the *townsfolk* *deeper*, the *line's* *broken'* — *changed* his *order*, *freed* him to *move.* …But the *rout* *took* me. *Bodies*, *moving*, *carrying* me *back* through the *sally-port* whether I *willed* it or *no*, and I — I *told* myself I'd *re-form* on the *road* and *come* *back* for them. *(hollow)* …We did not *re-form.* There *was* no *coming* *back.* They *ran* us *down* in the *open*, and I *died* in a *ditch* a *mile* off with *Bregan's* *name* in my *mouth* and his *order* *still* *standing.* *(quietly)* …So *that's* how a *relief* comes to be *un-sent*, cold one. Not *malice.* Not even *cowardice*, *quite.* Just — a *wall* that *broke* *faster* than a *promise* could *be* *kept*, and a *captain* who *meant* to *come* *back* and *didn't.* *(barely)* …*'Meant* to*'* is the *cruelest* *phrase* in any *tongue.* I have *worn* it *four* *hundred* *years.*

nodes.speaker: Captain Wystan
nodes.text: *(The question — why won't you cross — strikes deeper than any accusation, and for a moment the rehearsed control slips and you see the raw thing beneath.)* …*Three* *words.* *(barely)* …*'Watch* *relieved*, *soldier.'* *(he stares at the gate)* …Do you *think* I haven't — cold one, I have *started* across that *ground* a *thousand* *times.* *Ten* *thousand.* I *square* my *shoulders* and I *take* the *first* *step* and then I — *(he stops)* …Because to *give* the *order* — to *walk* up to that *steady*, *faithful*, *waiting* man and *say* the *words* — means he will *look* at me. *(his voice drops)* And in the *instant* before the *relief* *lands*, he will *understand.* He will *do* the *arithmetic* he has *refused* to *do* for *four* *hundred* *years* — *why* did the *relief* *take* *four* *centuries*? — and the *answer* will be *standing* *right* *in* *front* of *him*, in a *captain's* *colours*, and he will *know*: *no* one *forgot* him by *accident.* His *Captain* *lived* *long* *enough* to *give* the *order* and *never* *came.* *(anguished)* …I cannot *be* the one who puts *that* in his *face.* I would *rather* he *hold* the *gate* *forever*, *believing* the *relief* *fell* on the *road* *through* no *one's* *fault*, than *free* him with a *truth* that tells him his *Captain* *left* him to *rot* at a *post* for *four* *hundred* *years.* *(barely)* …*That's* why I *don't* *cross*, cold one. Not to *spare* *myself.* To *spare* *him* the *knowing.* …Or so I *tell* myself. Perhaps it's *both.* Perhaps the *kindest* *lie* and the *coward's* *lie* are, in the *end*, the *same* *hundred* *feet* *un-walked.*

nodes.speaker: Captain Wystan
nodes.text: *(He listens, rigid, and when the thing lands — that the order frees the giver too, that Bregan waits not to accuse but to be relieved — the four-hundred-year mask of rehearsed condemnation cracks, and under it is just a soldier who loved his men and failed one and has never forgiven himself the hour.)* …He's *waiting* for his *Captain.* *(barely)* …Not to — not to *damn* me. To be — *(his voice breaks on it)* …*relieved.* *(he looks at the gate, full on, the first time in your presence)* …You're *right.* *Gods* *help* me, you're *right*, and I've *known* it *under* all the *other* *knowing*: that my *staying* *apart* *frees* *no* *one.* It *keeps* *him* at the *post* and *keeps* *me* in the *cold* and *honours* *nothing* but my *own* *flinch.* *(he straightens, and something military and long-buried comes back into his bearing)* …I have spent *four* *hundred* *years* *guarding* him *from* a *truth* — and *calling* it *mercy* — when the *only* *mercy* he ever *needed* was the *order* I was *too* *much* a *coward* to *carry.* *(he takes a breath he does not need)* …*No* *more.* *(quietly, fierce)* …I'll *cross.* I'll *walk* the *hundred* *feet.* And I'll *give* my *guardsman* the *relief* I've *owed* him since the *wall* *came* *down* — and if he *does* the *arithmetic*, if he *looks* at me and *knows* — then I'll *stand* *there* and *let* him *know*, and *take* whatever's in his *face*, because a *captain* who *sent* a *man* to a *post* *owes* him the *courage* to *stand* *and* *be* *seen* when he *finally* *comes* to *fetch* him *off* it. *(barely)* …*Come* with me, cold one. *Stand* *witness.* I have not been *brave* in *four* *hundred* *years.* I'd — I'd *like* a *soul* to *see* me *try* it *once* more, *before* the *end.*

nodes.speaker: Captain Wystan
nodes.text: *(You tell him: Bregan feared the Captain fell on the road, and is glad he didn't, and is not angry, and waits to see him. And Wystan — who has armoured himself for four hundred years against the accusation he was certain would come — has no armour at all against this, and it goes through him like a blade.)* …He — *(he can barely speak)* …he *feared* *I* — *(his hand goes to his mouth)* …He's been *standing* at that *gate* for *four* *centuries* and the thing he *feared* was not that I *abandoned* him — but that I *FELL.* That I *died* trying to *reach* him. *(he makes a sound that is not quite a sob)* …*Gods.* *Gods*, cold one. I have spent *four* *hundred* *years* *certain* he must *hate* me — *building* my whole *guilt* on the *justice* of his *hatred* — and the *whole* time the *steady* *fool* was up there *worrying* *for* *me.* *Grieving* a *captain* he thought *died* in the *rout.* *(he turns toward the gate, and now nothing holds him back)* …*Not* angry. *(barely)* …He's *better* than I *am*, cold one. He always *was*. That's *why* I *trusted* him with the *last* *gate.* You give the *hardest* *post* to the *best* *man*, and then you spend *eternity* *unworthy* of *what* he *did* with it. *(he squares his shoulders, and the soldier returns entire)* …*Take* me to him. *Now*, before I *lose* the *nerve.* I have a *watch* to *relieve* and a *guardsman* to *thank* and a *forgiveness* I do *not* *deserve* to *accept* on my *knees* if I *must.* *(fierce, freed)* …*Four* *hundred* *years* *late*, cold one. But *no* longer. *Lead* *on.* The *Captain's* *coming*, Bregan. The *Captain's* *finally* *coming.*

nodes.speaker: Captain Wystan
nodes.text: *(He nods, and returns his gaze to the dark, the gate held forever at the edge of his sight, never met.)* …*Aye.* *Go.* *(quietly)* …I'll be *here.* I am *always* *here.* It is the *one* *post* I've *never* *abandoned* — *(bitterly)* — *this* one. The *watching* *of* *him.* From *too* *far* to *help* and *too* *close* to *forget.* *(barely)* …Cold one. *(as you turn)* …If you *mean* to *free* him — and I *think* you *might*, there's a thing in you that *gets* the *frozen* *moving* — then *do* it *well.* *Better* than *I* did *anything* that *last* *day.* *(quietly)* …And if there comes a *moment* where the *only* thing that *frees* him is *me* — the *order*, the *crossing*, the *facing* — *(he closes his eyes)* …*come* *get* me. Don't — don't let me *off* with my *flinching.* I have *hidden* in this *guilt* *four* *hundred* *years* because *guilt*, at *least*, *asks* *nothing* of you but to *keep* *feeling* it. *Drag* me to the *gate* if you *must*, cold one. I have *earned* the *dragging.* And he has *earned* a *Captain* who *finally* *shows* *up* — even if he has to be *hauled* there by a *cold* *dead* *hand.*
